source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 12.3 Security (pp. 211-213)

questions.q: If NEITHER /etc/cron.allow nor /etc/cron.deny exists, who can use cron?
questions.why: With neither file present, all users may use cron.

options: cron.deny is ignored - only users in cron.allow may use cron
questions.why: When cron.allow is present it is the sole authority; cron.deny is completely ignored.

questions.q: What does an EMPTY /etc/cron.allow mean, versus an empty /etc/cron.deny?
options: Empty cron.allow = NO users may use cron; empty cron.deny = ALL users may
Both empty files mean all users may use cron
Both empty files mean no users may use cron
Empty cron.allow = all users; empty cron.deny = no users
questions.why: An empty allow-list permits nobody; an empty deny-list forbids nobody (so everyone is allowed).

questions.q: What is the default situation on the system regarding these two files?
options: /etc/cron.deny exists and is empty; /etc/cron.allow does not exist (so everyone may use cron)
Both files exist and list root only
questions.why: By default cron.deny is present but empty and cron.allow is absent, so all users can use cron.

questions.q: If the SAME user appears in both /etc/cron.allow and /etc/cron.deny, what happens?
options: The user CAN use cron (allow wins)
The user cannot use cron (deny wins)
questions.why: cron.allow takes precedence, so a user listed in both is allowed to use cron.
